title: The Dark Tower I: The Gunslinger - The Ran(King) of King - Part 8
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4b4THjNuDW0

Daniel (@dungeondive) reaches the book he calls "the big one" in The Ran(King) of King — 1982's The Dark Tower I: The Gunslinger — confessing up front that he's a "tower junkie" and that Dark Tower titles will dominate the top of his list. Roland, the last gunslinger, pursues the man in black across a desert: we learn of the ill-fated town of Tull, his hawk-sacrifice coming-of-age, the boy Jake he lets fall for his quest, and a closing fireside conversation about the size of the universe.

He acknowledges it's the series' least-read, most divergent entry — written by a very young King still finding his voice, reading more like a Bachman book: mystical, cold, distant, dreamlike. He defends the later revisions as additions that connect it to the saga's end without George-Lucas-ing the original. His reading of the whole series is that it's about story itself — the ritual bond between teller and listener (Roland can't tell Brown his tale until asked), and humanity's need for narrative, a theme central to the channel. Not exciting, deliberately meandering, but for him immensely rewarding.

It goes straight to number one on the board.

----

Did The Gunslinger hook you or nearly lose you — and would you start a newcomer here or with book two?
